If no such weight exists, then ˜N(i) ⃗ w= ˜N(i+1) ⃗ w . This completes the proof of the second inequality in Proposition 4.2. At any iteration i, at best the number of learnable parameters remains constant, and at worst, it decreases. E Experimental Implementations Details In this section, we discuss all details behind our implemen- tations in Section 5. These include training recipes, tensor quantization speciﬁcs, and static quantization calibration details. All experiments were implemented using DGX-1 V olta machines. E.1 Baseline Training Recipes The following recipes were used for obtaining ImageNet training-from-scratch results included in Section 5.1. All models were trained using momentum SGD using 8 V100 GPUs. The following parameters are the defaults suggested in the ‘Deep Learning Examples’ repository4. ResNet training used the following parameters: an initial learning of 0.1, a per-GPU batch-size of 64, a momentum factor of 0.9, a weight decay factor of 1e-4, and a learning rate decay factor of 0.1 every 30 epochs. ResNet-50 and ResNet-18 were trained for 150 epochs, while ResNet-101 was trained for 80 epochs. MobileNet training used the following parameters: an ini- tial learning rate of 0.2, a per-GPU batch-size of 128, a momentum factor of 0.9, a learning weight decay factor of 0.1 every 100 epochs, and a total number of training epochs of 300. Weight decay factors of 4e-5 and 1e-5 were used for MobileNet-V2 and MobileNet-V3, respectively. Shortened versions of the above recipes were used for re- training discussed in Section 5.2. Furthermore, models were retrained using 4 V100 GPUs. More speciﬁcally, ResNets and MobileNets were retrained using an initial learning rate of 1e-4 and 1e-2, respectively. ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 were retrained for 15 epochs with a learning rate decay of 0.1 every 5 epochs. All other models were retrained for 30 epochs with a learning rate decay of 0.1 every 10 epochs. For ﬁne-tuning of BERT models on Squad, we used 1 V100 GPU and referred to the default parameters suggested in the ‘Deep Learning Examples’ repository 5. Speciﬁcally, we ﬁne-tune for 2 epochs and use: a batch of 4, a learning rate 4Code retrieved from https://github.com/NVIDIA/ DeepLearningExamples/tree/master/PyTorch/ Classification/ConvNets. 5Code retrieved from https://github.com/NVIDIA/ DeepLearningExamples/tree/master/PyTorch/ LanguageModeling/BERT. of 3e-5, and a maximum sequence length of 384. E.2 Tensor Quantization Speciﬁcs It is important to explicitly describe various options for quan- tization granularity. The following are commonly employed methods: • Tensor scaling, where one scalar sis used to quantize all elements in a tensor ⃗t. • Per-output-channel (POC) scaling , which applies to weights in convolutional layers. A convolutional weight tensor ⃗ whas a dimensionality of (K,C,R,S ) where K and C are the output and input number of channels, re- spectively, and Rand Sare the kernel height and width, respectively. A vector ⃗ sof Kscalars is used to quantize C×R×Selements at a time. Speciﬁcally, the tensor ⃗ wis ﬂattened into a matrix of dimensionality (K,C ×R×S) and every row vector is quantized using the scalar in ⃗ sof appropriate index. • Per-output-feature (POF) scaling , which applies to weights in linear (fully connected) layers. A weight ma- trix ⃗ whas dimensionality (O,I) where Oand I are the output and input number of features, respectively. A vec- tor ⃗ sof Oscalars is used to quantize the row vectors of ⃗ w in a similar fashion as the POS scaling case. The advantage of POS and POF scaling is the ﬁner granu- larity of quantization, which can boost accuracy. The above three methods are well-adapted to GPU and deep learning accelerator datapaths (Wu et al., 2020). In addition, the overhead of quantization metadata is small enough that in our work we assume all scalars to be in full precision (Dai et al., 2021). In our experiments, we use POC scaling for convolutional weight quantization in all ResNets and MobileNets, and POF scaling for the weight layers in BERT models. Tensor scaling is used for all other tensors, which include: activa- tions and fully connected layers in ResNets and MobileNets, and activations in BERT models. For the last, we deﬁne activations as being inputs to the various linear layers in the transformer blocks, as well as inputs to the Batch Matrix Multiply (BMM) operations. Finally, we note that ﬁrst (convolutional) and last (fully connected) layers in ResNets and MobileNets are always quantized to 8-bit, as is customary for such networks (Choi et al., 2018). However, projection layers (also known short- cut connections) are quantized to low precision (4-bit, 6-bit, and 8-bit, depending on the experiment). E.3 Static Quantization Calibration Details In this section, we describe the methodology to calibrate clipping scalars for retraining and ﬁne-tuning experimentsOptimal Clipping and Magnitude-aware Differentiation for Improved Quantization-aware Training in Section 5.2 and 5.3, respectively. Weight calibration is done by simply using the pretrained weights at various layers and computing various clipping scalar candidates. Speciﬁcally, the candidates used are the OCTA V and MSE sweep predicted scalars, as well as the 99.9th, 99.99th, and 99.999th percentiles. For activations, we sample 5 random input batches from the training set. For each, we compute the various candidates above at every layer. Thus, for each strategy, we obtain a collection of 5 candidates for every activation tensor. The average of these candidates is used as a calibrated clipping scalar. For BERT-Base, we sample and average over 10 random inputs from the training set. We deﬁne the MSE Sweep as being a 100-point sweep. Speciﬁcally, for every tensor, we evaluate the sweep over {k 100 ·smax}100 k=1. In the case of POC and POF scaling, we run the sweep over{k 100 ·⃗ smax}100 k=1 where ⃗ smax is the vector of row-wise maxima in the tensors. This is an approximation leading to a weaker solution than OCTA V , which, thanks to broadcasting, can perform row-wise optimization with no overhead. Nonetheless, we ﬁnd both to yield similar results, as highlighted in our results in Section 5.2. F OCTA V vs. Brute Force Sweep Speed Comparison We compare times taken to calibrate BERT-Base tensors when using OCTA V vs. the MSE sweep. The calibration was done on an Intel Xeon CPU, using the NumPy package, and no code optimization was performed for either. We choose to measure time on a CPU to provide the fairest and most accurate comparison possible. Indeed, on a GPU, signiﬁcant noise in time measurement can occur due to various communication of data between GPU and host that may be required. As OCTA V only requires tensor operations, its GPU speedup over MSE sweeps is expected to be even greater than what we report here. In BERT-Base, there are 74 weight layers and thus 74 weight tensors to be processed. For each of these layers, we also process an input activation tensor. As we use 10 random in- put batches, we obtain 740 activation tensors to process. Fur- thermore, there are 24 BMM operations, each of which has two operands that are added to the list of activations. Thus, we get another 48 activation tensors as BMM operands per input batch, for a total of 480 additional activation tensors. Thus, we process 1220 activation tensors overall. As we perform calibration for B = 4,5,6,7,8, we report average times per-precision. For each tensor, we start a timer as soon as we invoke our calibration (OCTA V vs. MSE sweep) routine and stop it as soon as execution terminates. Our measurements are listed in Table 6. We ﬁnd that OC- Table 6: Calibration times comparison of OCTA V vs. MSE Sweep for BERT-Base Weight Activation Per-Tensor Avg. (seconds) OCTA V 0.191 0.497 MSE Sweep 1.943 3.141 Total Calib. (h:mm:ss) OCTA V 0:00:14 0:10:06 MSE Sweep 0:02:24 1:03:52 Speed-up 10.2× 6.3× TA V processes one weight tensor in 0.191 sec., on average completing all weight calibration in only 14 sec. In contrast, the MSE sweep requires over 2 mins. and is 10.2 ×slower. For activations, OCTA V processes one tensor in 0.497 sec. on average, and completes all activation calibration in just over 10 mins. This corresponds to the calibration of 1220 tensors on a CPU. In contrast, the MSE sweep is 6.3 × slower and requires over 1 hour to terminate. Interestingly, activation calibration speed-up is less pronounced than that of weights. We speculate that this is due to the activation tensors being much larger so that much of the execution time goes into data movement from memory, which amortizes the speed-up. G When is MSE not Convex? Comparing static quantization using OCTA V vs. MSE sweep results for BERT models in Section 5.3 reveals unex- pected results. As shown in Table 5, OCTA V calibration at a low precision yields a signiﬁcantly higher accuracy than the MSE sweep. As both methods are supposed to return similar solutions, we investigated the reason of this discrepancy. In Figure 5(a) we plot the calibrated clipping scalar for acti- vations (linear layers input only, BMM operands excluded) as a function of layer index when using OCTA V and the MSE sweep. We observe that the solution is identical almost everywhere, except at a few layers. Speciﬁcally, at layers 28, 33, 40, and 64, the OCTA V-calibrated scalar is noticeably smaller than its MSE sweep counterpart. To understand this phenomenon, we carefully compared the data at layers 63 and 64. For the former, the two calibrated strategies con- verge to the same solution, but for the latter, OCTA V returns a clipping scalar of ∼8 while the MSE sweep returns ∼38. In Figure 5(b,c), we plot the empirical probability distribu- tion function (PDF) of the tensors used for calibration at layer 63 and 64, respectively. These tensors correspond to the 10 random input batches selected for calibration, each of which is represented by one color. A stark contrast in distribution is observed: layer 63 appears to be typical with most of the density concentrated around zero, while layer 64 data has large outliers around the value 350. In Figure 5(d,e), we plot the 4-bit clipped quantization MSE as a function of clipping scalars for layers 63 and 64, re-Optimal Clipping and Magnitude-aware Differentiation for Improved Quantization-aware Training 0 10 20 30 40 50 60 70 80 Layer Index 0 10 20 30 40 50 60Calibrated Clipping Scalar MSE Sweep OCTAV (a) 4  3  2  1  0 1 2 3 4 Activation Value 10-4 10-3 10-2 10-1 100 101 PDF Layer #63 (b) 400  300  200  100  0 100 200 300 400 Activation Value 10-6 10-5 10-4 10-3 10-2 10-1 100 PDF Layer #64 (c) MSE is convex and OCTAV  converges to the global optimum (d) OCTAV  converges to the  first minimum Quantization noise of outliers is larger  than total variance of small data (e) Figure 5: Investigation of 4-bit clipped quantization MSE con- vexity in BERT-Base: (a) calibrated clipping scalars returned by OCTA V and MSE sweep for activation layers, (b) PDF of activa- tion tensors at layer 63, (c) PDF of activation tensors at layer 64, (c) MSE vs. clipping scalar at layer 63, and (d) MSE vs. clipping scalar at layer 64. Different colors correspond to the 10 different input batches used for calibration. Activation tensors considered are input to the linear layers in BERT-Base, while BMM operands are not shown. spectively. We also pinpoint the predicted clipping scalar by OCTA V for each of the 10 tensors. As expected, for layer 63, the MSE is a convex function and OCTA V accurately con- verges to its global optimum. In contrast, layer 64 exhibits a more complex behavior. The MSE does include a local minimum close to zero, which corresponds to the trade-off between discretization and clipping noise, and to which OC- TA V converges to. Beyond that point, the MSE increases as the clipping scalar increases, and this is due to an increase in the discretization step. At some point, non-outlier data is bound to become smaller than the magnitude of the least signiﬁcant bit and thus be quantized to zero. At this point, the quantization noise related to non-outlier data quantiza- tion is equal to its total variance and no longer increases when the clipping scalar increases. However, with larger clipping scalar, the quantization noise of outliers themselves decreases, which leads to the MSE decreasing again. The MSE therefore has a second minimum, which is close to the maximum scalar and only caters for outlier representation. Furthermore, for one of the 10 tensors processed, in this case, the one corresponding to the green line in Figure 5(d), the MSE at this second minimum is smaller than that of the ﬁrst one. This second minimum is thus selected by the MSE sweep and skews the calibrated scalar towards the outliers. In conclusion, when tensors have large outliers, the MSE sweep may return a calibrated scalar that caters only for outliers and zeroes out all small values. In contrast, OCTA V converges to the minimum closest to zero, which balances discretization and clipping noise of all data in the tensor. This is in fact the only minimum identiﬁed by J(s) in (4), due to the additive noise model assumption. Thus, OCTA V is guaranteed to converge to this ﬁrst minimum. Intuitively, the solution returned by OCTA V is desired, as it caters for all the data in the tensor. This intuition concurs with our experimental results in Section 5.3. Indeed, static-OCTA V was found to yield a noticeably higher accuracy than the MSE sweep for both BERT models at low precision (4-to-6- bit).

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces OCTAV (Optimally Clipped Tensors And Vectors), a fast Newton–Raphson-based recursive algorithm that computes mean-squared-error (MSE) optimal clipping thresholds per tensor at every QAT iteration; provides theoretical analysis showing convexity and optimality under typical conditions. Identifies fundamental issues with common gradient estimators for clipped quantization: STE can cause gradient explosion and PWL can cause partial convergence stoppage. Proposes magnitude-aware differentiation (MAD) and a MAD–PWL hybrid (MPH) to improve stability and accuracy. Demonstrates state-of-the-art low-precision (4–6 bit) QAT results on ImageNet with ResNets and MobileNets and on SQuAD fine-tuning with BERT, with no changes to baseline training recipes beyond inserting quantizers. Also proposes static-OCTAV for fast, accurate calibration and shows ~10× speedup over brute-force MSE sweeps.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Clipped uniform quantization: formulates MSE for clipped quantizers, balancing discretization and clipping error. Derives signed/unsigned formulations.
- OCTAV: Uses Newton–Raphson to solve for MSE-optimal clipping scalar s*. Recursive update (empirical form): s_{n+1} = sum(|x|·1{|x|>s_n}) / [ (4^{-B}/3)·sum(1{0<|x|≤s_n}) + sum(1{|x|>s_n}) ] for signed data (analogous unsigned form). Requires only tensor ops (abs, compare, masks, reductions); converges in ~10 iterations; insensitive to initialization; broadcastable for per-subtensor scaling (POC/POF).
- Gradient estimation: analyzes STE (proves exponential gradient variance growth with depth) and PWL (proves monotonic decrease in number of learnable weights under static clipping). Proposes MAD: dQ/dx = 1 for |x|≤s and s/|x| for |x|>s; continuous and avoids PWL’s stagnation. Recommends MPH: MAD for weights, PWL for activations (regularization-like effect).
- Static-OCTAV calibration: applies OCTAV offline on pretrained weights and sampled activations; compares with percentile (99.9/99.99/99.999) and 100-point MSE sweep; shows OCTAV faster and more accurate, especially in presence of outliers where MSE can exhibit multiple minima.
- Implementation: PyTorch tensor ops; per-output-channel scaling for conv weights, per-output-feature for linear weights; first/last layers kept at 8-bit; same baseline training recipes; theoretical proofs and empirical validation of MSE model and optimality.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Datasets/Tasks: ImageNet classification with ResNet-18/50/101, MobileNet-V2, MobileNet-V3 Small/Large; SQuAD v1.1 question answering with BERT-Base and BERT-Large (fine-tuning).
- Protocols: Training-from-scratch and retraining (short and long) for ImageNet; fine-tuning for SQuAD. Bit-widths: 4, 6, 8 (and 5,7 for BERT study). First/last layers at 8-bit; POC scaling for conv weights; POF scaling for linear weights; tensor scaling for activations.
- Baselines/Comparisons: Full-precision baseline; max-scaling quantization; dynamic-OCTAV vs static calibration (static-OCTAV, 100-point MSE sweep, percentile 99.9/99.99/99.999). Gradient estimators compared: STE, PWL, MAD, MPH.
- Metrics: ImageNet Top-1 accuracy; SQuAD F1.
- Calibration: Weights from pretrained models; activations from multiple sampled batches (5 for ImageNet, 10 for BERT). Static calibration via OCTAV, MSE sweep, percentiles.
- Efficiency study: CPU timing for BERT-Base calibration shows OCTAV ~10× faster than MSE sweep for weights and ~6.3× for activations (overall >10× for weights, >6× for activations). Hardware/software: DGX-1 with V100 GPUs; PyTorch.
- Key results: 4-bit ResNet-50 with OCTAV+MPH within <1% of baseline; across ResNets, 4-bit training/retraining near-baseline; MobileNet-V2 strong at 4–6 bit with OCTAV; MobileNet-V3 requires OCTAV to converge, still with some drop at 4-bit; BERT fine-tuning: dynamic-OCTAV best across 4–8 bits, static-OCTAV superior to sweep/percentiles, especially with outliers.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- OCTAV adds runtime overhead each iteration (though far lower than brute-force sweeps), which may hinder deployment or very large-scale training; not optimized for minimal overhead.
- Assumes an additive noise model in discretization region; rare non-convex MSE scenarios arise with heavy-tailed outliers, where empirical MSE has multiple minima; OCTAV converges to the local minimum near zero (often preferable), but not the global minimum of empirical MSE in such cases.
- Focuses on uniform quantization; non-uniform formats not implemented though conceptually extendable.
- Gradient estimator derivations rely on piecewise-linear approximations (indicator derivatives) and do not provide full convergence guarantees for all network/training settings.
- Static quantization performs poorly for small/mobile models; dynamic quantization may be needed during retraining, complicating deployment.
- Does not integrate advanced QAT-specific training techniques (e.g., distillation) or address fully quantized training (FQT) of gradients/updates; energy/latency measurements not reported.
- Some architectures (e.g., MobileNet-V3 at 4-bit) still show noticeable accuracy drops without additional recipe changes.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Combine OCTAV with QAT-friendly training strategies such as knowledge distillation, teacher–student methods, or data augmentation tailored for low precision to close remaining accuracy gaps (e.g., MobileNet-V3 at 4-bit).
- Reduce OCTAV runtime overhead via approximations, low-cost proxies, or learned predictors to enable faster dynamic quantization and make static calibration match dynamic accuracy.
- Extend the Newton–Raphson online optimization approach to alternative fidelity objectives (Lp norms, KL divergence) and to non-uniform quantizers (log, floating-point, custom formats).
- Apply the methodology to fully quantized training (FQT) including gradients and optimizer states; investigate stability and convergence with MAD/MPH under FQT.
- Develop robust clipping/optimization strategies for heavy-tailed/outlier distributions (e.g., robust M-estimators, outlier-aware objectives) to handle non-convex empirical MSE.
- Explore hardware–software co-design: per-subtensor scaling policies, metadata compression, and deployment strategies for inference with static calibration that preserves OCTAV’s accuracy.
- Generalize the Newton–Raphson on-the-fly optimization framework to other hardware-aware objectives (e.g., sparsification, pruning) and integrate into NAS for efficient, low-precision architectures.

new_method.method:
Title: SketchBank: Forward-only, Quantization-native On-device Learning via Jacobian Sketch Banks

Motivation
- Problem focus (edge-first): Tiny MCUs/NPUs (8–32 MB SRAM, tight power budgets) cannot afford on-device backprop, activation checkpoints, or QAT loops. Even PEFT baselines still need gradients or large activation buffers, and quantized models degrade or behave inconsistently during personalization.
- Why now: (a) Low-bit inference is widespread on-device; (b) privacy and regulatory constraints discourage raw-data uplink; (c) recent results show quantization-activated failures (Qu-ANTI-zation) that current on-device adaptation ignores; (d) OCTAV-style clipping control and spectral PEFT show where most “leverage” is, but still assume gradient flows during adaptation.
- Gap: No method provides a secure, forward-only, memory-minimal, quantization-native personalization pipeline that adapts both classical PEFT knobs (bias/norm/spectral) and explicit quantization controls (per-channel scales/clips) with FP–int8 parity monitoring. Prior art either keeps inner-loop gradients (TinyTL, SVFT, Spectral Adapter, FedL2P) or offloads data/cloud (U‑VPA), or assumes a full QAT graph (OCTAV).

Methodology
Core idea
- Replace on-device backprop with precomputed, quantized “Jacobian-to-adapter” maps trained offline. Devices do a single forward pass, form a tiny error feature e′, and apply int8 matvecs to update micro-adapters (bias, norm affine, quant scales/clips, and r≤16 spectral diagonals). No autograd, no activation saves, batch size 1.

What’s new
1) Adapter mix expressly designed for forward-only, low-bit personalization
- Bias and normalization affine: TinyTL-like capacity, but updates come from forward-only maps instead of gradients.
- Quantization adapters (first-class citizens): per-channel weight/activation scales and clipping thresholds are updated from e′ using an OCTAV-lite surrogate of the Newton step, but realized via pre-fit int8 maps (no QAT graph on device).
- Spectral-diagonal adapters: store frozen int8 U,V for a few layers; update only r diagonal entries (r≤16) to approximate SVFT-like capacity with hundreds–thousands of params and forward-only control.
- Joint control: a single forward-only loop co-adapts bias/norm, spectral diagonals, and quantization scales/clips—unexplored in prior PEFT.

2) SketchBank (server-side precomputation → int8 package for device)
- Error projector P∈R^{C×r_e} (r_e=8–16): learned from public/proxy data by SVD of logit/grad-covariance; class-balanced variants cover tails. Quantized to int8 with per-column scales.
- Per-adapter group maps S_g: using auto-diff offline, compute J_g(x) and fit S_g to satisfy J_g^T e ≈ S_g P^T e with ridge and Fisher preconditioning; quantize S_g rows to int8.
- Mixture-of-sketches: K small sketches per group; a 16–32-width gate predicts w_k(x) from cheap signals (BN-KL, entropy, feature energy), improving robustness to drift with constant on-device cost.
- OCTAV-lite training targets: compute near-Newton Δs*,Δc* for scales/clips under an MSE objective; fit S_q mapping e′→(Δs,Δc), enabling low-bit fidelity without QAT on-device.
- Packaging: {P, S_g^(k)} int8 matrices + int8 U,V for selected layers + per-tensor scales; total 0.5–1.5 MB for 5–20k adaptive parameters.

3) On-device forward-only adaptation loop (batch size 1)
- Forward: compute logits y. Loss choices: (i) supervised CE when labels exist, (ii) entropy minimization, (iii) EMA-teacher consistency, or (iv) self-distillation. Form e (e.g., softmax(y)−onehot or ∂H/∂logits), then e′=P^T e.
- Update: for each group g, Δθ_g=−η_g Σ_k w_k(x) S_g^(k) e′. Implement as int8×fp16 matvecs on DP4A/NEON. Fuse updates in-place; no activation buffering.
- Stabilizers: trust-region scaling by EMA(||e′||), per-group Δθ clipping, momentum EMA, and short-horizon accept/reject with rollback.
- Task-general heads: provide head-specific P for detection/KWS/NLP intent via the same API. Cost ≈ O(p_total·r_e); e.g., r_e=16, p_total≈10k → ~1.6e5 MACs/step.

4) Quantization parity + integrity guards
- FP–int8 parity monitors: periodic fake-quant forwards at bits∈{8,6,4}; if FP–int gap exceeds a learned threshold, attenuate η_g or freeze the implicated groups.
- Sketch integrity checks: random-sign checksum and synthetic e′ challenge–response after download; quarantine on anomalies.
- DP-lite telemetry (optional): upload only a few DP-noised scalars (EMA ||e′||, BN-KL, scale drift) under secure aggregation; never upload raw images.

5) Ultra-light cloud collaboration (optional)
- Uplink: a few scalars per layer every T steps; secure aggregation to refine S_g/P/gate.
- Downlink: periodic int8 deltas (<1 MB) to track new domains/clients. No image uplink (unlike U‑VPA).

6) Theory + diagnostics
- Approximation bound: E||J_g^T e − S_g P^T e|| ≤ projection error(P) + fit error(S_g) + drift term; suggests r_e and K selection.
- Trust-region accept/reject guarantees non-increasing expected loss under bounded e′ noise.

How it differs from prior work
- TinyTL/BN+Last: removes on-device backprop entirely; no activation storage; adds quantization adapters as first-class variables.
- FedFomo/FedL2P: avoids inner-loop gradients/meta-hypergrads; personalization comes from pre-fit sketch maps; cloud sees only DP-noised scalars.
- SVFT/Spectral Adapter: achieves spectral capacity with forward-only diagonal updates and pre-fit maps; no gradient flow on device, and includes quantization-control adaptation.
- OCTAV: reuses Newton-style targets but avoids a QAT graph on-device; provides parity/integrity guards missing in PEFT/QAT.
- U‑VPA: no image uplink; adapts without teacher images.
- Qu‑ANTI-zation: parity monitors + quarantine mitigate quantization-activated behaviors during adaptation.

Minimal system changes to existing codebases
- Library API
  - SketchBank(P,S,scales).project_error(e) → e′; SketchBank.apply_update(adapters, e′, gate) → in-place int8×fp16 matvec updates.
  - Adapters: BiasNormAdapter, QuantScaleCalibrator, SpectralDiagonalAdapter with momentum/rollback.
  - Gate: tiny MLP (16–32 hidden) consuming shift stats.
- Kernels: DP4A/NEON-optimized int8×fp16 matvecs; per-row scales fused.
- Device loop: forward → compute e → e′ → apply_update → parity check → accept/rollback.
- Offline trainer: scripts to learn P, S_g^(k), gate, OCTAV-lite S_q from proxy/public data; emits an int8 “sketch package”.

Evaluation plan (edge-first)
- Models/tasks: ResNet‑18/MobileNet‑V2 on CIFAR‑100/DomainNet; MobileNet‑SSD (detection); DS‑CNN (KWS); DistilBERT (intent). 8/6/4-bit variants.
- Baselines: TinyTL, BN+Last, LoRA/SVFT/Spectral, full fine-tuning, and static calibration. When applicable, include OCTAV QAT.
- Metrics: accuracy/mAP; FP–int8 gap over time; on-device latency/energy per step; SRAM footprint; uplink/downlink budget; rate/efficacy of parity-triggered throttling; Qu‑ANTI attack suites.
- Ablations: r_e, K, adapter mix, trust-region thresholds, spectral vs bias/norm-only, inclusion of scale/clip updates, gate features.

Expected impact
- Practical on-device continual learning: collapses adaptation to a single forward and a few int8 matvecs, fitting 8–32 MB SRAM devices and mobile NPUs while preserving privacy.
- Robust low-bit personalization: explicitly adapts quantization scales/clips alongside PEFT parameters, maintaining accuracy at 8/6/4-bit under drift.
- Security by design: FP–int8 parity monitoring and sketch integrity checks reduce attack surfaces exposed by quantization artifacts.
- General primitive: a drop-in substrate for CNNs/Transformers across vision, speech (KWS), and lightweight NLP intents with sub-megabyte overhead.
- New PEFT design point: backprop-free, memory-minimal, quantization-native, and cloud-collaborative without raw-data sharing—complementing and advancing beyond TinyTL, meta-personalization (FedL2P/FedFomo), and spectral PEFT.

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
Title: Experimental Plans for SketchBank: Forward-only, Quantization-native On-device Learning via Jacobian Sketch Banks

Overview
- We propose three complementary experiments that jointly validate SketchBank’s effectiveness: accuracy/latency/memory on vision domain shift (Exp-1), quantization-control stress tests and parity safeguards (Exp-2), and cross-modality generalization on speech KWS (Exp-3).
- All experiments are designed to be implemented in Python with PyTorch; they simulate on-device constraints (batch=1, no backward pass), use int8 matrix-vector kernels, and measure memory/latency.
- Each experiment includes baselines, metrics, ablations, and success criteria.

Common components (used across experiments)
1) Model quantization (PTQ)
   - Use simple per-channel weight quantization (symmetric, int8) and per-tensor activation quantization with optional clipping. Fall back to PyTorch fake-quant for parity checks at 8/6/4 bits.
2) Sketch package (offline): P, S_g^(k), optional int8 U,V for spectral-diagonal adapters, and S_q for scale/clip.
3) On-device loop (forward-only):
   - Compute logits y; loss proxy L (supervised CE or unsupervised entropy/consistency) -> error e.
   - Project e' = P^T e (r_e ≤ 16).
   - Update adapters with int8 × fp16 matvecs: Δθ_g = −η_g Σ_k w_k(x) S_g^(k) e'. Apply trust region, momentum, and rollback if parity guard fails.
4) Parity monitor: periodic fake-quant forward at bits∈{8,6,4}; if FP–int gap > τ_g, attenuate η_g or freeze group g.

Python scaffolding (minimal, shared)
- Dependencies: torch, torchvision/torchaudio, numpy, scipy.
- All code snippets below are minimal and can be extended; data/model specifics are referenced per experiment.

Code: Core primitives

class Int8RowQuant:
    def __init__(self, W_fp32, per_row=True, eps=1e-8):
        # Quantize row-wise to int8 with per-row scale
        W = W_fp32.detach().cpu().float()
        if per_row:
            s = W.abs().amax(dim=1, keepdim=True) / 127.0 + eps
            Q = torch.clamp((W / s).round(), -127, 127).to(torch.int8)
            self.scale = s.squeeze(1).numpy()  # per-row scale
        else:
            s = W.abs().amax() / 127.0 + eps
            Q = torch.clamp((W / s).round(), -127, 127).to(torch.int8)
            self.scale = float(s)
        self.Q = Q
        self.per_row = per_row

    def matvec(self, x_fp16):
        # int8xfp16 matvec: y = (Q * diag(scale)) @ x
        x = x_fp16.detach().float()
        if self.per_row:
            # scale rows after int8 matvec
            y_int = torch.matmul(self.Q.float(), x)
            y = y_int * torch.from_numpy(self.scale).to(y_int)
        else:
            y = torch.matmul((self.Q.float() * self.scale), x)
        return y

class SketchBank:
    def __init__(self, P_fp32, S_groups, gate):
        # P: C x r_e (float32 for projection accuracy, or int8-packed if desired)
        self.P = P_fp32  # torch.Tensor
        # S_groups: dict[group_name] = list of Int8RowQuant (K sketches)
        self.S_groups = S_groups
        self.gate = gate  # callable: x-> weights over K

    def project_error(self, e):
        # e: C-vector (float32)
        return torch.matmul(self.P.t(), e)  # r_e-vector

    def apply_update(self, adapters, e_prime, gate_feats):
        # adapters: dict[group_name] -> Adapter object with step(Δθ)
        w = self.gate(gate_feats)  # torch.Tensor [K]
        for g, S_list in self.S_groups.items():
            # Compute Σ_k w_k S_g^(k) e'
            upd = 0.0
            for k, Sk in enumerate(S_list):
                yk = Sk.matvec(e_prime.half())  # fp32 out
                upd = upd + w[k] * yk
            adapters[g].step(upd)

class MomentumAdapter:
    def __init__(self, params_ref, lr=0.1, mom=0.9, clip=0.1):
        self.ref = params_ref  # a list of torch.Tensors to update in-place
        self.lr, self.mom, self.clip = lr, mom, clip
        self.v = [torch.zeros_like(p) for p in self.ref]
    def step(self, delta_flat):
        # Unflatten delta_flat into param-shaped tensors
        offset = 0
        for i, p in enumerate(self.ref):
            n = p.numel()
            d = delta_flat[offset:offset+n].view_as(p)
            offset += n
            d = torch.clamp(d, -self.clip, self.clip)
            self.v[i] = self.mom * self.v[i] + (1 - self.mom) * d
            p.data.add_(-self.lr * self.v[i])

class GateMLP(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, K):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = torch.nn.Sequential(
            torch.nn.Linear(in_dim, 32), torch.nn.ReLU(),
            torch.nn.Linear(32, K)
        )
        self.K = K
    def forward(self, feats):
        logits = self.net(feats)
        w = torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
        return w

# Trust region + parity guard (simplified)
def parity_guard(model, x, bits_list=[8,6,4], tau=0.02):
    with torch.no_grad():
        y_fp = model.forward_fp(x)
        for b in bits_list:
            y_q = model.forward_fakequant(x, bits=b)
            gap = (y_fp - y_q).abs().mean().item()
            if gap > tau:
                return False, gap
    return True, 0.0


Experiment 1: Vision domain shift personalization (MobileNetV2 on CIFAR-100-C)
Goal and hypothesis
- Goal: Show that SketchBank achieves near-gradient PEFT accuracy with much lower memory/latency and robust low-bit behavior during on-device adaptation.
- Hypothesis: With r_e≤16 and 10–20k adaptive parameters, SketchBank approaches TinyTL accuracy while cutting peak memory by >10× and sustaining 8/6/4-bit parity via quantization adapters and parity guards.

Dataset and models
- Base pretraining: Train MobileNetV2 on CIFAR-100 train set (float32 or 8-bit-aware PTQ). Use torchvision or timm MobileNetV2.
- Target domain: CIFAR-100-C (common corruptions) at severities 1–5. Test sequential online adaptation with batch=1.
- Quantization: PTQ to int8 and a simulated 6/4-bit via fake-quant. Use per-channel weight and per-tensor activation quant.

Adapters and groups
- g1: Bias and BN affine parameters across all conv/BN layers.
- g2: Spectral-diagonal adapters on the last two stages (store U,V offline; update only r=8–16 diagonals per layer).
- g3: Quantization adapters: per-channel weight scale s_w and activation clip c_a for the last two stages and classifier.
- Total adaptive params target: 8–20k.

Offline precomputation (proxy data: CIFAR-100 train)
1) Learn error projector P ∈ R^{C×r_e}:
   - For CE loss, sample e_i = softmax(y_i)-onehot, compute empirical covariance of e and take top r_e singular vectors (class-balanced sampling).
2) Fit S_g maps per group g (K=1,4):
   - For sampled inputs x and one-hot labels, compute J_g^T e by autograd wrt adapter params (no need to store activations globally; this is offline on workstation).
   - Fit S_g to minimize Σ ||J_g^T e − S_g P^T e||^2 + λ||S_g||^2. Solve by ridge regression per group. Quantize S_g row-wise to int8 (Int8RowQuant).
3) OCTAV-lite targets for quantization adapters:
   - For each quant tensor, numerically estimate near-Newton updates Δs*, Δc* under MSE loss on proxy batches; fit S_q as above mapping e' to (Δs,Δc).
4) Gate training (optional K>1):
   - Train GateMLP to predict sketch index weights from cheap features (layer entropy, BN-KL divergence, feature energy). Loss: L2 between Σ_k w_k S_k e' and J^T e on held-out proxy samples.
5) Package size budget: ensure total sketch matrices + U,V + scales ≤ 1.5 MB.

On-device loop (simulation)
- Setup adapters:
    adapters = {
        'bias_bn': MomentumAdapter(list_of_bias_and_bn_params, lr=0.3, mom=0.9, clip=0.05),
        'spectral': MomentumAdapter(list_of_diag_params, lr=0.2, mom=0.9, clip=0.05),
        'quant': MomentumAdapter(list_of_scales_and_clips, lr=0.05, mom=0.9, clip=0.02),
    }
- Gate: GateMLP(in_dim=6, K) taking features: [entropy(y), ||x||2, BN-KL stage3, BN-KL stage4, logit margin, running ||e'||].
- Online adaptation (per sample):
    for x,y in stream:
        y_logits = model.forward_quant(x)  # int8 path
        e = torch.softmax(y_logits, dim=-1) - torch.nn.functional.one_hot(y, 100).float()
        e_prime = sketchbank.project_error(e)
        # Trust-region scaling by ema_norm
        ok, gap = parity_guard(model, x, tau=0.02)
        if not ok:
            # attenuate LR or freeze quant group
            adapters['quant'].lr *= 0.5
        sketchbank.apply_update(adapters, e_prime, gate_feats)
        # Optional short-horizon accept/reject: evaluate on x again; rollback if loss increased

Baselines
- Static PTQ (no adaptation).
- BN+Last (gradient-based, batch=1; store only BN stats and last layer grads/activations).
- TinyTL (bias+norm) gradient-based on-device.
- Spectral adapter or LoRA (small rank) with gradients.
- OCTAV QAT inner-loop (if feasible) as an upper-bound reference (offline or with tiny steps).

Metrics
- Top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100-C, averaged across severities and corruption types.
- Accuracy vs online steps (after each sample or after N=100 steps).
- FP–int8 parity gap over time; number of parity-triggered throttles/freezes.
- On-device simulated latency per step (Python wall-time), and MAC-count estimate.
- Peak memory: torch.cuda.memory_allocated() if GPU or tracemalloc on CPU.
- SRAM-equivalent footprint of sketch package.

Ablations
- r_e ∈ {8, 16}, K ∈ {1, 4}, adapter mixes: {bias_bn}, {bias_bn+spectral}, {bias_bn+quant}, {all}.
- Disable trust-region vs enable.
- With/without gate features.

Success criteria
- Accuracy: ≥90% of TinyTL’s gain over static PTQ at 8-bit; clear advantage at 6/4-bit due to quant adapters.
- Memory/latency: ≥10× less peak memory than TinyTL and ≤0.5 ms per sample (CPU estimate) for update at p≈10k, r_e=16.
- Parity: reduced FP–int8 gap drift; parity guard triggers correlate with corruption severity and stabilize training.


Experiment 2: Quantization-control stress test and parity safeguards
Goal and hypothesis
- Goal: Demonstrate that SketchBank’s quantization adapters (s_w, c_a) and parity monitors mitigate quantization-activated failures under calibration drift or adversarial conditions.
- Hypothesis: With only quantization adapters enabled, SketchBank recovers most of the lost accuracy caused by miscalibration and reduces FP–int8 gap, while parity guards prevent catastrophic updates.

Setup
- Use the same MobileNetV2 + CIFAR-100-C as Exp-1, but induce controlled quantization failures:
  1) Miscalibration: perturb per-channel weight scales by ±10–20% and activation clips by −30% (too tight) on last two stages.
  2) Shifted input energy: apply global brightness/contrast changes and shot noise; simulate heavy-tailed activations.
  3) Optional Qu-ANTI-style triggers: introduce synthetic outlier patches to provoke activation saturation.

Adapters and loop
- Enable only g3 (quantization adapters). Freeze bias_bn and spectral.
- Use unsupervised loss when labels unavailable: entropy minimization L = Σ_i p_i log p_i; e = ∂L/∂logits = p (1 + log p).
- OCTAV-lite S_q maps trained offline as in Exp-1.
- Parity guard at tighter tau (e.g., 0.01); implement automatic η_g attenuation on repeated violations, and short-horizon rollback.

Baselines
- Static PTQ (no adaptation).
- BN-only adaptation (gradient) to show it cannot fix quant failures.
- Full QAT fine-tune of scales/clips on a small buffer (upper bound, not on-device), for 1–2 epochs.

Metrics
- Top-1 accuracy before perturbation, after perturbation, and after adaptation (per corruption/severity).
- FP–int8 parity gap trajectories; count of parity-triggered freezes and their effect on stability.
- Distribution of activation max vs clip before/after (coverage metric: fraction within 0.8×clip).
- Time-to-recover (steps needed to reach 95% of pre-perturb accuracy).

Ablations
- Compare S_q trained with MSE targets vs. naive proportional control on s_w and c_a.
- r_e ∈ {8,16}, K ∈ {1,4} for quant group only.
- Trust-region off vs on.

Success criteria
- Recovery: ≥70% of the accuracy lost due to miscalibration recovered within ≤500 steps (batch=1).
- Parity: FP–int8 gap reduced by ≥50% vs no-adaptation; parity guard prevents divergence under severe shifts.

Sketch code snippet (adapter-only loop)

for x in stream:
    y = model.forward_quant(x)
    p = torch.softmax(y, dim=-1)
    e = p * (1.0 + torch.log(torch.clamp(p, 1e-6, 1.0)))  # entropy-grad surrogate
    e_prime = sketchbank.project_error(e)
    ok, gap = parity_guard(model, x, bits_list=[8,6,4], tau=0.01)
    if not ok:
        adapters['quant'].lr *= 0.5
    sketchbank.apply_update({'quant': adapters['quant']}, e_prime, gate_feats)


Experiment 3: Cross-modality validation on speech KWS (DS-CNN on SpeechCommands)
Goal and hypothesis
- Goal: Show modality generality and spectral-diagonal effectiveness under tiny parameter budgets.
- Hypothesis: SketchBank with bias/norm + small spectral diagonals (r≤8) improves KWS under noisy domain shift with minimal latency and no backprop.

Dataset and model
- SpeechCommands v2 (35 classes). Base model: DS-CNN (small) pre-trained.
- Domain shift: Add background noise (MUSAN) and room impulse responses (RIRs) to test stream; SNR ∈ {0, 5, 10} dB.
- Quantization: int8 weights, 8-bit activations; evaluate 6-bit simulated.

Adapters
- g1: Bias + BN affine in conv blocks.
- g2: Spectral-diagonal on final conv block (store U,V offline; update r=8 diagonals).
- g3: Quantization adapters for first conv activation clip and final dense layer scales.
- Unsupervised loss: confidence sharpening via entropy minimization; optional EMA-teacher consistency.

Offline precompute
- P from logits error covariance on clean SpeechCommands train.
- S_g learned on proxy audio augmentations (noise/RIR) to improve robustness.

On-device loop
- Batch=1 streaming; 20–40 ms frames; apply update every frame or every N frames.
- Gate features: log-mel energy variance, entropy, BN-KL.

Baselines
- Static PTQ; BN-only gradient update; TinyTL (bias+BN) gradient update.

Metrics
- Accuracy/F1 across SNRs; false positive rate for “unknown/silence” classes.
- Per-frame adaptation latency; MAC estimate; memory footprint.
- FP–int8 parity gap and parity-triggered throttling counts.

Ablations
- Adapter mixes; r_e ∈ {8,16}; r (spectral) ∈ {4,8,16}; K ∈ {1,4}.

Success criteria
- ≥3–5% absolute accuracy gain over static PTQ at 5 dB SNR with ≤0.3 ms update latency and ≤1 MB sketch package.


Implementation details and reproducibility
- Seeding: torch.manual_seed(1234), numpy seed.
- Compute: Single CPU or a modest GPU; offline S_g fitting uses autograd and ridge (can be done on GPU for speed).
- Energy proxy: compute MACs for updates as p_total × r_e and convert to nJ/MAC (~1–3 nJ on mobile CPU) for rough energy estimates.
- Memory: Count model parameters kept in SRAM during update (adapters), sketch matrices, and temporary buffers.

Offline sketch fitting (reference code skeleton)

def learn_P(train_loader, model, C, r_e=16):
    Es = []
    with torch.no_grad():
        for x,y in train_loader:
            ylog = model.forward_fp(x)
            p = torch.softmax(ylog, dim=-1)
            e = p - torch.nn.functional.one_hot(y, C).float()
            Es.append(e.cpu())
    E = torch.cat(Es, dim=0)
    U,S,Vt = torch.linalg.svd(E, full_matrices=False)
    P = U[:,:r_e]  # C x r_e
    return P.contiguous()

def fit_S_group(train_loader, model, adapters_flat, P, lam=1e-3, K=1):
    # Build design matrix Z = P^T e and targets T = J^T e (via autograd)
    Zs, Ts = [], []
    for x,y in train_loader:
        x, y = x.to('cuda'), y.to('cuda')
        ylog = model.forward_fp(x)
        p = torch.softmax(ylog, dim=-1)
        e = p - torch.nn.functional.one_hot(y, p.size(-1)).float().to(p)
        e_prime = (P.t().to(p) @ e.t()).t()  # batch x r_e
        # Compute J^T e for adapter params (flattened)
        loss = (p * torch.log(torch.clamp(p,1e-6,1.0))).sum(dim=-1).mean()  # placeholder; use CE if labels
        grads = torch.autograd.grad(loss, adapters_flat, retain_graph=False, allow_unused=True)
        gflat = torch.cat([g.reshape(-1) if g is not None else torch.zeros_like(p.reshape(0)) for g in grads])
        Zs.append(e_prime.cpu())
        Ts.append(gflat.detach().cpu())
    Z = torch.cat(Zs, dim=0)  # N x r_e
    T = torch.stack(Ts, dim=0)  # N x p
    # Ridge per-row: solve S in argmin ||Z S^T - T||^2 + lam||S||^2
    # Closed-form: S = (Z^T Z + lam I)^(-1) Z^T T
    ZtZ = Z.t() @ Z
    A = ZtZ + lam * torch.eye(ZtZ.size(0))
    S = torch.linalg.solve(A, Z.t() @ T)  # r_e x p
    S = S.t().contiguous()  # p x r_e (rows map to adapter params)
    S_int8 = Int8RowQuant(S)
    return S_int8


Reporting
- Provide per-experiment CSV logs for accuracy, latency, memory, parity metrics.
- Include sketch package size and adapter parameter count in reports.

Risks and mitigations
- If 4-bit parity checks trigger frequently, clamp η_g and reduce adapter mix to quant-only until stability returns.
- If fitting S_g is unstable, increase lam or apply Fisher preconditioning using empirical Fisher diag during offline fit.

Expected outcomes summary
- Exp-1: SketchBank reaches TinyTL-like gains with substantially lower memory and maintains performance at low bits.
- Exp-2: Quantization adapters repair miscalibration quickly and parity guards prevent catastrophic drift.
- Exp-3: Benefits transfer to speech; small spectral capacity plus bias/norm yield notable gains under noise with tiny overhead.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
This document elaborates each experimental item in the Experiment Strategy for SketchBank. It includes practical PyTorch-oriented procedures, reliability measures, and executable code sketches that minimize from-scratch implementation and leverage standard libraries.

Common components (shared across experiments)
- Tooling and dependencies
  - Python ≥3.9, PyTorch ≥2.2, torchvision, torchaudio (for KWS), numpy, scipy, pandas, tqdm. Optional: torch.ao.quantization, torchmetrics, torch.profiler, torch.backends.cudnn for determinism. For audio augmentation: torch-audiomentations (optional), or torchaudio SoX effects.
  - Seeding and determinism: set seeds for torch, numpy; enable cudnn.deterministic (when using GPU); disable TF32 for fair FP vs fake-quant parity.

- Quantization and model wrapper (PTQ primary; fake-quant for parity at 6/4-bit)
  - We will use torch.ao.quantization for int8 PTQ of conv/linear with per-channel weight quant and per-tensor activation quant where supported (fbgemm backend on CPU). For parity checks at bits∈{8,6,4}, we add FakeQuantize modules configured with quant_min/max corresponding to bit width.

Code: model quantization and parity-capable wrapper

class QuantParityWrapper(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, fp_model, num_classes, calibr_loader, device='cpu'):
        super().__init__()
        self.fp_model = fp_model.to(device).eval()
        self.C = num_classes
        self.device = device
        # Prepare PTQ int8 model (per-channel weights if available)
        self.qconfig = torch.ao.quantization.get_default_qconfig('fbgemm')
        self.quant_model = copy.deepcopy(fp_model).to(device).eval()
        self.quant_model.qconfig = self.qconfig
        torch.ao.quantization.prepare(self.quant_model, inplace=True)
        with torch.no_grad():
            for xb, _ in calibr_loader:
                self.quant_model(xb.to(device))
        torch.ao.quantization.convert(self.quant_model, inplace=True)
        # Fake-quant model for parity checks at lower bits
        self.fq_models = {}
        for b in [8, 6, 4]:
            fq = copy.deepcopy(fp_model).to(device).eval()
            # Insert fake-quant observers to simulate b-bit
            # We assume a helper applies FakeQuantize modules globally
            apply_fake_quant(fq, bitwidth=b)
            self.fq_models[b] = fq

    def forward_fp(self, x):
        with torch.no_grad():
            return self.fp_model(x)

    def forward_quant(self, x):
        with torch.no_grad():
            return self.quant_model(x)

    def forward_fakequant(self, x, bits=8):
        with torch.no_grad():
            return self.fq_models[bits](x)


def apply_fake_quant(model, bitwidth=8):
    # Replace activation and weight observers with FakeQuantize configured to given bitwidth
    # Uses torch.ao.quantization.fake_quantize; minimal version applied to Conv/Linear modules
    qmin = -(2 ** (bitwidth - 1))
    qmax = 2 ** (bitwidth - 1) - 1
    for m in model.modules():
        if isinstance(m, (torch.nn.Conv2d, torch.nn.Linear)):
            m.weight_fake_quant = torch.ao.quantization.FakeQuantize(
                observer=torch.ao.quantization.MovingAverageMinMaxObserver,
                quant_min=qmin, quant_max=qmax, dtype=torch.qint8,
                qscheme=torch.per_tensor_symmetric)
            # For activations
            m.activation_post_process = torch.ao.quantization.FakeQuantize(
                observer=torch.ao.quantization.MovingAverageMinMaxObserver,
                quant_min=qmin, quant_max=qmax, dtype=torch.quint8,
                qscheme=torch.per_tensor_affine)


- Adapters and SketchBank core
  - BiasNormAdapter: wraps references to biases and BN affine parameters. Updates performed in-place, with momentum and clipping.
  - SpectralDiagonalAdapter: offline stores U,V (int8) for selected layers after SVD on flattened weights. On-device, maintains small diagonal vector d (r entries) and applies low-rank update to the weight tensor after each step. Re-computation is once per update step (not per forward). U,V stored as int8 with per-row scaling; updates computed in fp16.
  - QuantScaleCalibrator: maintains per-channel weight scaling multipliers and per-tensor activation clip corrections for selected layers. Operates by modifying dequant scales or fake-quant clip parameters in quantized/fq paths. Does not require re-quantizing int8 Q tensors on device; only adjusts scale variables and activation clamp thresholds.
  - SketchBank: holds projector P (float32 or int8+scale) and per-group row-quantized S_g^(k) matrices, plus a tiny gate MLP.

Code: int8 row-quantization, gate, adapters, spectral update

class Int8RowQuant:
    def __init__(self, W_fp32: torch.Tensor, eps=1e-8):
        W = W_fp32.detach().cpu().float()
        s = W.abs().amax(dim=1, keepdim=True) / 127.0 + eps
        Q = torch.clamp((W / s).round(), -127, 127).to(torch.int8)
        self.Q = Q
        self.scale = s.squeeze(1).contiguous()  # per-row scale

    def matvec(self, x_fp16: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        x = x_fp16.detach().float()
        y_int = torch.matmul(self.Q.float(), x)
        return y_int * self.scale


class GateMLP(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, K):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = torch.nn.Sequential(
            torch.nn.Linear(in_dim, 32), torch.nn.ReLU(),
            torch.nn.Linear(32, K)
        )
    def forward(self, feats):
        return torch.softmax(self.net(feats), dim=-1)


class MomentumAdapter:
    def __init__(self, params_ref, lr=0.1, mom=0.9, clip=0.1):
        self.ref = params_ref
        self.lr, self.mom, self.clip = lr, mom, clip
        self.v = [torch.zeros_like(p) for p in self.ref]
    def flatten_params(self):
        return torch.cat([p.detach().reshape(-1) for p in self.ref])
    def _unflatten(self, flat: torch.Tensor):
        offset = 0
        for i, p in enumerate(self.ref):
            n = p.numel()
            d = flat[offset:offset+n].view_as(p)
            offset += n
            yield i, p, d
    def step(self, delta_flat):
        offset = 0
        for i, p, d in self._unflatten(delta_flat):
            d = torch.clamp(d, -self.clip, self.clip)
            self.v[i].mul_(self.mom).add_((1 - self.mom) * d)
            p.data.add_(-self.lr * self.v[i])


class BiasNormAdapter(MomentumAdapter):
    pass


class SpectralDiagonalAdapter:
    def __init__(self, layers, U_pack, Vt_pack, r, lr=0.2, mom=0.9, clip=0.05):
        # layers: list of Conv2d modules to augment
        # U_pack, Vt_pack: dict[layer_id] -> (U_int8, scale_row), (Vt_int8, scale_row)
        self.layers = layers
        self.U_pack = U_pack
        self.Vt_pack = Vt_pack
        self.r = r
        self.lr, self.mom, self.clip = lr, mom, clip
        self.diag = {id(l): torch.zeros(r, device=next(l.parameters()).device) for l in layers}
        self.v = {id(l): torch.zeros_like(self.diag[id(l)]) for l in layers}
        # Cache base weights for efficient low-rank update
        self.baseW = {id(l): l.weight.data.clone() for l in layers}

    def _dequant(self, Q, scale):
        return Q.float() * scale.view(-1, 1)

    def step(self, delta_concat):
        # delta_concat is concatenated over all layers' r diagonals
        offset = 0
        for l in self.layers:
            key = id(l)
            d = delta_concat[offset:offset+self.r]
            offset += self.r
            d = torch.clamp(d, -self.clip, self.clip)
            self.v[key].mul_(self.mom).add_((1 - self.mom) * d)
            self.diag[key].add_(-self.lr * self.v[key])
            # Apply low-rank update to l.weight: W = W0 + U diag(diag) Vt
            UQ, Us = self.U_pack[key]
            VQ, Vs = self.Vt_pack[key]
            U = self._dequant(UQ, Us)[:, :self.r]    # [out, r]
            Vt = self._dequant(VQ, Vs)[:self.r, :]   # [r, in*k*k]
            upd = U @ (self.diag[key].unsqueeze(1) * Vt)  # [out, in*k*k]
            upd = upd.view_as(l.weight)
            l.weight.data.copy_(self.baseW[key] + upd)


class QuantScaleCalibrator(MomentumAdapter):
    # Expects a list of leaf tensors representing: per-channel weight scales and activation clips
    # For PyTorch sim: store as nn.Parameter tensors referenced in forward dequant/fake-quant
    pass


class SketchBank:
    def __init__(self, P_fp32: torch.Tensor, S_groups: dict, gate: GateMLP, ema_beta=0.99):
        self.P = P_fp32.contiguous()
        self.S_groups = S_groups  # dict[str] -> list[Int8RowQuant]
        self.gate = gate
        self.ema_beta = ema_beta
        self.ema_norm = 1.0

    def project_error(self, e: torch.Tensor):
        e_prime = self.P.t() @ e  # r_e vector
        # Update EMA for trust region
        with torch.no_grad():
            self.ema_norm = self.ema_beta * self.ema_norm + (1 - self.ema_beta) * (e_prime.norm().item() + 1e-8)
        return e_prime

    def apply_update(self, adapters: dict, e_prime: torch.Tensor, gate_feats: torch.Tensor):
        w = self.gate(gate_feats)
        for g, S_list in self.S_groups.items():
            upd = 0.0
            for k, Sk in enumerate(S_list):
                yk = Sk.matvec(e_prime.half())
                upd = upd + w[k] * yk
            # Trust region scaling
            scale = min(1.0, 1.0 / (1e-6 + self.ema_norm))
            adapters[g].step(scale * upd)


- Gate features and parity guard
  - Gate features: we compute at runtime cheap signals: entropy of logits, logit margin (top1−top2), feature energy (||x||2 or a mid-layer feature), BN-KL proxies (difference in running means/vars vs calibration), and running ||e′||.
  - Parity guard: periodic FP vs fake-quant forwards at bits∈{8,6,4}; if gap>τ, attenuate learning rate or freeze implicated groups; optional rollback.

Code: gate features and parity guard

def compute_gate_feats(model, x, y_logits, e_prime, bn_layers=None):
    with torch.no_grad():
        p = torch.softmax(y_logits, dim=-1)
        entropy = -(p * (p.clamp_min(1e-6).log())).sum().unsqueeze(0)
        top2 = torch.topk(p, k=2).values
        margin = (top2[0] - top2[1]).unsqueeze(0)
        x_energy = x.float().pow(2).mean().unsqueeze(0)
        bnm = []
        if bn_layers is not None:
            for bn in bn_layers:
                # KL proxy: abs diff of running mean/var normalized
                bnm.append((bn.running_mean.abs().mean() + bn.running_var.abs().mean()).unsqueeze(0))
        bnm = torch.stack(bnm, dim=0).mean().unsqueeze(0) if bnm else torch.tensor([0.0])
        e_norm = torch.tensor([e_prime.norm().item()])
        feats = torch.cat([entropy, margin, x_energy, bnm, e_norm])
        return feats


def parity_guard(model_wrap: QuantParityWrapper, x, bits_list=[8,6,4], tau=0.02):
    with torch.no_grad():
        y_fp = model_wrap.forward_fp(x)
        bad = False
        worst_gap = 0.0
        for b in bits_list:
            y_q = model_wrap.forward_fakequant(x, bits=b)
            gap = (y_fp - y_q).abs().mean().item()
            worst_gap = max(worst_gap, gap)
            if gap > tau:
                bad = True
        return (not bad), worst_gap


- Offline sketch fitting and packaging
  - Compute P via SVD on logit error vectors e = softmax(y) − one_hot(y) (for CE supervision). To be class-balanced, stratify sampling per class.
  - Compute per-group J_g^T e using autograd with custom backward injection: autograd.grad(y_logits, params, grad_outputs=e) (no explicit loss; this computes Jacobian^T times vector e). Concatenate adapter params into a single flat target vector per sample.
  - Fit S_g via ridge regression: solve S_g in argmin ||Z S_g^T − T||^2 + λ||S_g||^2 where Z = P^T e and T stacks J_g^T e across samples. Quantize rows to int8.
  - OCTAV-lite for quantization adapters: generate near-Newton targets Δs*,Δc* via finite differences or closed-form approximations on proxy batches; fit S_q similarly.
  - Train gate (if K>1) to minimize L2 error between predicted Σ_k w_k S_k e′ and J_g^T e on held-out proxy data.

Code: learning P and S_g (autograd-based), class-balanced sampling

def class_balanced_loader(dataset, batch_size=128, samples_per_class=200):
    from collections import defaultdict
    cls_idx = defaultdict(list)
    for i, (_, y) in enumerate(dataset):
        cls_idx[int(y)].append(i)
    idx = []
    for c, arr in cls_idx.items():
        idx.extend(arr[:samples_per_class])
    subset = torch.utils.data.Subset(dataset, idx)
    return torch.utils.data.DataLoader(subset, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True, num_workers=2)


def learn_P(model, loader, C, r_e=16, device='cuda'):
    E = []
    model.eval().to(device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        for xb, yb in loader:
            xb, yb = xb.to(device), yb.to(device)
            ylog = model(xb)
            p = torch.softmax(ylog, dim=-1)
            e = p - torch.nn.functional.one_hot(yb, C).float()
            E.append(e.cpu())
    E = torch.cat(E, dim=0)  # N x C
    U, S, Vt = torch.linalg.svd(E, full_matrices=False)
    P = U[:, :r_e]  # N x r_e; we want C x r_e, so recompute on covariance of e per class
    # Better: directly SVD on empirical covariance in class space
    Cov = (E.t() @ E) / (E.size(0) - 1)  # C x C
    Uc, Sc, Vtc = torch.linalg.svd(Cov, full_matrices=False)
    P = Uc[:, :r_e].contiguous()  # C x r_e
    return P


def jacobian_T_e(model, xb, e_vec, group_params):
    # Compute J^T e: vector-Jacobian product wrt group_params
    model.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
    ylog = model(xb)
    grads = torch.autograd.grad(ylog, group_params, grad_outputs=e_vec, retain_graph=False, allow_unused=True)
    gflat = []
    for g in grads:
        if g is None:
            gflat.append(torch.zeros(0, device=xb.device))
        else:
            gflat.append(g.reshape(-1))
    return torch.cat(gflat)


def fit_S_group(model, loader, P, group_params, lam=1e-3, K=1, device='cuda'):
    model.eval().to(device)
    Zs, Ts = [], []
    for xb, yb in loader:
        xb, yb = xb.to(device), yb.to(device)
        ylog = model(xb)
        p = torch.softmax(ylog, dim=-1)
        e = p - torch.nn.functional.one_hot(yb, p.size(-1)).float()
        # For each sample in batch, accumulate
        for i in range(xb.size(0)):
            ei = e[i]
            e_prime = P.t().to(device) @ ei  # r_e
            Zs.append(e_prime.detach().cpu().unsqueeze(0))
            t = jacobian_T_e(model, xb[i:i+1], ei.unsqueeze(0), group_params)
            Ts.append(t.detach().cpu().unsqueeze(0))
    Z = torch.cat(Zs, dim=0)  # N x r_e
    T = torch.cat(Ts, dim=0)  # N x p
    # Solve ridge: S (p x r_e) = (Z^T Z + lam I)^{-1} Z^T T, then transpose appropriately
    ZtZ = Z.t() @ Z
    A = ZtZ + lam * torch.eye(ZtZ.size(0))
    S = torch.linalg.solve(A, Z.t() @ T)  # r_e x p
    S = S.t().contiguous()  # p x r_e
    return Int8RowQuant(S)


- OCTAV-lite targets for quant adapters
  - For each quantized tensor, approximate near-Newton update of scale s and clip c by minimizing batch MSE between pre-quant and dequant outputs y and ŷ(s,c). Closed-form approximations exist for uniform quantization; otherwise use small step finite-difference gradients and diag Hessian approximations.

Code: coarse OCTAV-lite target fitting for a single tensor

def octav_lite_targets(model, loader, affected_modules, P, device='cuda'):
    # Returns Int8RowQuant S_q mapping e' -> delta over concatenated [s_w, c_a]
    Zs, Ts = [], []
    model.eval().to(device)
    for xb, yb in loader:
        xb, yb = xb.to(device), yb.to(device)
        ylog = model(xb)
        p = torch.softmax(ylog, dim=-1)
        e = p - torch.nn.functional.one_hot(yb, p.size(-1)).float()
        for i in range(xb.size(0)):
            ei = e[i]
            e_prime = P.t().to(device) @ ei  # r_e
            Zs.append(e_prime.detach().cpu().unsqueeze(0))
            # Build near-Newton target per module (concat across modules)
            deltas = []
            for mod in affected_modules:
                # Example: take small FD steps on scale s and clip c to minimize activation MSE
                s = mod.weight_scale.clone().detach().requires_grad_(True)
                c = mod.act_clip.clone().detach().requires_grad_(True)
                # Simplified surrogate loss: MSE between fake-quant outputs with (s,c) vs teacher FP
                y_fp = model.forward_fp(xb[i:i+1]) if hasattr(model, 'forward_fp') else ylog[i:i+1]
                y_fq = forward_with_scales(model, xb[i:i+1], mod, s, c)
                loss = torch.nn.functional.mse_loss(y_fq, y_fp)
                g_s, g_c = torch.autograd.grad(loss, [s, c])
                # Diagonal Hessian approx via squared gradients or second FD; take Newton-like step
                h_s = (g_s.detach().pow(2).mean() + 1e-6)
                h_c = (g_c.detach().pow(2).mean() + 1e-6)
                ds = - (g_s / h_s).detach()
                dc = - (g_c / h_c).detach()
                deltas.extend([ds.view(-1).cpu(), dc.view(-1).cpu()])
            Ts.append(torch.cat(deltas).unsqueeze(0))
    Z = torch.cat(Zs, dim=0)
    T = torch.cat(Ts, dim=0)
    ZtZ = Z.t() @ Z
    A = ZtZ + 1e-3 * torch.eye(ZtZ.size(0))
    S = torch.linalg.solve(A, Z.t() @ T)  # r_e x p
    S = S.t().contiguous()
    return Int8RowQuant(S)


- Integrity checks and secure packaging
  - Integrity checksum: random-sign checksum on each int8 matrix row; verify on device at load.
  - Challenge–response: device generates random e′ vectors and expects S_g P^T e to match within tolerance; otherwise quarantine.

Code: simple checksum

def sign_checksum(Q: torch.Tensor, seed=0):
    g = torch.Generator().manual_seed(seed)
    s = torch.randint_like(Q, low=-1, high=2, generator=g)
    return int((Q.int() * s.int()).sum().item())


Experiment 1: Vision domain shift personalization (MobileNetV2 on CIFAR-100-C)
- Goal and hypothesis
  - Validate that SketchBank (forward-only) approaches TinyTL accuracy with far lower memory/latency and maintains robustness at 8/6/4 bits through quantization adapters and parity safeguards.

- Dataset and models
  - Base: torchvision.models.mobilenet_v2 pretrained on CIFAR-100 (train from scratch or finetune ImageNet-pretrained head). Use standard 32×32 pipeline (resize or adapt stem).
  - Target domain: CIFAR-100-C corruptions at severities 1–5 (https://github.com/hendrycks/robustness). Stream samples by corruption type and severity.

Code: data preparation

def get_cifar100_loaders(batch_size=128, num_workers=4):
    tf_train = torchvision.transforms.Compose([
        torchvision.transforms.RandomCrop(32, padding=4),
        torchvision.transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip(),
        torchvision.transforms.ToTensor(),
    ])
    tf_test = torchvision.transforms.ToTensor()
    train = torchvision.datasets.CIFAR100(root='./data', train=True, download=True, transform=tf_train)
    test = torchvision.datasets.CIFAR100(root='./data', train=False, download=True, transform=tf_test)
    train_loader = torch.utils.data.DataLoader(train, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True, num_workers=num_workers)
    test_loader = torch.utils.data.DataLoader(test, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=False, num_workers=num_workers)
    return train, test, train_loader, test_loader


def get_cifar100c_stream(root='./data/CIFAR-100-C', severities=(1,2,3,4,5)):
    # Returns an iterator yielding (x, y, corruption_name, severity)
    import numpy as np
    tf = torchvision.transforms.ToTensor()
    xs, ys, corrs, sevs = [], [], [], []
    for corr in os.listdir(root):
        if not corr.endswith('.npy'):
            X = np.load(os.path.join(root, corr))  # N x 32 x 32 x 3
            y = np.load(os.path.join(root, 'labels.npy'))
            N = X.shape[0]
            for s in severities:
                start = (s-1) * (N//5)
                end = s * (N//5)
                for i in range(start, end):
                    xs.append(tf(Image.fromarray(X[i])))
                    ys.append(int(y[i]))
                    corrs.append(corr)
                    sevs.append(s)
    for x, y, c, s in zip(xs, ys, corrs, sevs):
        yield x.unsqueeze(0), torch.tensor([y]), c, s


- Offline precomputation
  - Train/fine-tune MobileNetV2 on CIFAR-100 (float), then prepare PTQ model with calibration loader.
  - Learn P (r_e=16) via class-balanced SVD on training set.
  - Define adapter groups:
    - g1: all bias terms in Conv2d/Linear and BN affine (bn.weight, bn.bias).
    - g2: spectral-diagonal on last two MobileNetV2 stages (bottleneck blocks). Choose r=8–16 per layer. Offline compute SVD on flattened weights, store top-r singular vectors U,V (quantized to int8 row-wise).
    - g3: quantization adapters for per-channel weights in last two stages + classifier and per-tensor activation clips for those stages.
  - Fit S_g for g1, g2, and S_q for g3 using fit_S_group and octav_lite_targets. Use ridge λ in [1e-3, 1e-2]. Use K∈{1,4} by clustering samples according to gate features (k-means) and fitting per-cluster S, or by random partitions; then train GateMLP to interpolate.

Code: building parameter lists for groups

def collect_bias_bn_params(model):
    ps = []
    for m in model.modules():
        if isinstance(m, torch.nn.Conv2d) and m.bias is not None:
            ps.append(m.bias)
        if isinstance(m, torch.nn.BatchNorm2d):
            ps.extend([m.weight, m.bias])
        if isinstance(m, torch.nn.Linear) and m.bias is not None:
            ps.append(m.bias)
    return ps


def spectral_prep(model, stages, r=8):
    # stages: list of modules to spectrally adapt
    U_pack, Vt_pack = {}, {}
    for m in stages:
        W = m.weight.data.clone().float()
        out_c, in_c, kh, kw = W.shape
        W2d = W.view(out_c, -1)  # [out, in*kh*kw]
        # Compute compact SVD offline
        U, S, Vt = torch.linalg.svd(W2d, full_matrices=False)
        U_r = U[:, :r]
        Vt_r = Vt[:r, :]
        # Quantize rows
        Uq = Int8RowQuant(U_r)
        Vtq = Int8RowQuant(Vt_r)
        U_pack[id(m)] = (Uq.Q, Uq.scale)
        Vt_pack[id(m)] = (Vtq.Q, Vtq.scale)
    return U_pack, Vt_pack


- On-device loop (simulation)
  - Initialize QuantParityWrapper with calibration loader.
  - Instantiate adapters: BiasNormAdapter, SpectralDiagonalAdapter, QuantScaleCalibrator.
  - Build SketchBank with P and S_g^(k), and GateMLP.
  - Online per-sample (batch=1): forward int8; compute e; e′ = P^T e; gate_feats; parity check; apply updates; optional rollback.

Code: main adaptation loop and rollback

def clone_adapter_state(adapters):
    snap = {}
    for name, ad in adapters.items():
        if isinstance(ad, SpectralDiagonalAdapter):
            snap[name] = {k: v.clone() for k, v in ad.diag.items()}
        else:
            snap[name] = [p.data.clone() for p in ad.ref]
    return snap


def restore_adapter_state(adapters, snap):
    for name, state in snap.items():
        ad = adapters[name]
        if isinstance(ad, SpectralDiagonalAdapter):
            for k in ad.diag.keys():
                ad.diag[k].data.copy_(state[k])
                # Re-apply weight update after restoring diag
                ad.step(torch.zeros_like(ad.diag[k]))  # no-op step to refresh weights
        else:
            for p, s in zip(ad.ref, state):
                p.data.copy_(s)


def run_stream_adaptation(model_wrap, sketchbank, adapters, gate, stream_iter, C=100, tau=0.02):
    logs = []
    for x, y, corr, sev in stream_iter:
        x, y = x.to(model_wrap.device), y.to(model_wrap.device)
        ylog_q = model_wrap.forward_quant(x)
        p = torch.softmax(ylog_q, dim=-1).squeeze(0)
        e = p - torch.nn.functional.one_hot(y.squeeze(0), C).float().to(p)
        e_prime = sketchbank.project_error(e)
        feats = compute_gate_feats(model_wrap.fp_model, x, ylog_q, e_prime)
        ok, gap = parity_guard(model_wrap, x, bits_list=[8,6,4], tau=tau)
        snap = clone_adapter_state(adapters)
        if not ok:
            # attenuate quant adapter LR
            if 'quant' in adapters:
                adapters['quant'].lr *= 0.5
        sketchbank.apply_update(adapters, e_prime, feats)
        # Short-horizon accept/reject: if current sample loss increases or parity worsens, rollback
        ylog_q2 = model_wrap.forward_quant(x)
        loss_before = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(ylog_q, y.squeeze(0))
        loss_after = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(ylog_q2, y.squeeze(0))
        ok2, gap2 = parity_guard(model_wrap, x, bits_list=[8,6,4], tau=tau)
        if (loss_after > loss_before + 1e-3) or (gap2 > gap + 0.01):
            restore_adapter_state(adapters, snap)
        acc = (ylog_q2.argmax(dim=-1) == y).item()
        logs.append({'corr': corr, 'sev': int(sev), 'acc': acc, 'gap': gap2})
    return logs


- Baselines
  - Static PTQ: no adaptation. Evaluate once per stream.
  - BN+Last (gradient-based): enable grad only for BN affine and classifier bias; standard SGD with batch=1; store minimal activations.
  - TinyTL (bias+norm) gradient-based: enable grad for biases and BN affine across layers; single SGD step per sample.
  - Spectral/LoRA (gradient-based): insert small-rank adapters; update via SGD.
  - OCTAV QAT (upper bound): offline fine-tune scales/clips on small buffer with QAT for reference, not on-device.

Code: TinyTL-style baseline (bias+BN) with batch=1

def tiny_tl_step(model, x, y, lr=0.1):
    model.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
    logits = model(x)
    loss = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits, y)
    loss.backward()
    with torch.no_grad():
        for n, p in model.named_parameters():
            if ('bias' in n) or ('bn' in n and (n.endswith('weight') or n.endswith('bias'))):
                if p.grad is not None:
                    p.add_(-lr * p.grad)


- Metrics and logging
  - Accuracy per corruption and severity; mean and 95% CI over multiple randomized passes (≥3 seeds).
  - FP–int8 parity gap trajectories and number of throttles/freezes.
  - Latency: per-sample update wall-time (time.perf_counter) averaged; also compute MACs ≈ p_total × r_e.
  - Memory: sketch package size (sum of int8 matrices and scales), adapter parameter count; peak RAM via tracemalloc on CPU.
  - Energy proxy: update_MACs × nJ_per_MAC (1–3 nJ/MAC) with sensitivity analysis.

Code: basic logging to CSV

def log_to_csv(rows, path):
    import pandas as pd
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    df.to_csv(path, index=False)


- Ablations
  - r_e ∈ {8, 16}, K ∈ {1, 4}.
  - Adapter mixes: {bias_bn}, {bias_bn+spectral}, {bias_bn+quant}, {all}.
  - Trust-region off vs on; gate features on vs off (uniform w).

- Reliability enhancements
  - Repeat each configuration with ≥3 seeds; report mean±std and 95% CI (bootstrap over per-sample accuracies or per-corruption means).
  - Stratify stream order to avoid bias: rotate corruption types per seed.
  - Pre-register success criteria; use non-overlapping validation (do not tune on CIFAR-100-C severity=5 when reporting across all severities).

- Success criteria
  - Accuracy: achieve ≥90% of TinyTL’s gain over static PTQ at 8-bit; clear advantage at 6/4-bit due to quant adapters.
  - Memory/latency: ≥10× less peak memory than TinyTL, and ≤0.5 ms/update at p≈10k, r_e=16 (simulated CPU).
  - Parity stability: reduced FP–int8 gap drift; parity guard triggers correlate with severity and stabilize updates.


Experiment 2: Quantization-control stress test and parity safeguards
- Goal and hypothesis
  - Show that quantization adapters (s_w, c_a) and parity monitors repair miscalibration and mitigate quantization-activated failures; parity guard prevents catastrophic updates.

- Setup
  - Same MobileNetV2+CIFAR-100-C; reuse SketchBank P and S_q. Enable only g3 (quant adapters). Freeze others.
  - Induced failures:
    1) Miscalibration: perturb per-channel weight scales by ±10–20% and activation clips by −30% on last two stages.
    2) Shifted input energy: global brightness/contrast scaling and shot noise.
    3) Qu-ANTI triggers: add bright outlier patches (e.g., random 4×4 white squares) with low probability to provoke saturation.

Code: apply miscalibration and input perturbations

def perturb_scales_and_clips(model_wrap, target_layers, scale_pct=0.2, clip_pct=-0.3):
    for m in target_layers:
        if hasattr(m, 'weight_scale'):
            with torch.no_grad():
                m.weight_scale.mul_(1.0 + scale_pct * (2*torch.rand_like(m.weight_scale)-1))
        if hasattr(m, 'act_clip'):
            with torch.no_grad():
                m.act_clip.mul_(1.0 + clip_pct)


def augment_brightness_contrast(x, alpha=1.2, beta=0.1):
    # x in [0,1]
    return torch.clamp(alpha * x + beta, 0.0, 1.0)


def add_shot_noise(x, lam=50.0):
    # Poisson noise approx
    noisy = torch.poisson(x * lam) / lam
    return torch.clamp(noisy, 0.0, 1.0)


def add_outlier_patches(x, prob=0.1, patch=4):
    if torch.rand(1).item() < prob:
        B, C, H, W = x.shape
        i = torch.randint(0, H-patch, (1,)).item()
        j = torch.randint(0, W-patch, (1,)).item()
        x[:, :, i:i+patch, j:j+patch] = 1.0
    return x


- On-device loop
  - Unsupervised loss: entropy minimization; e = p (1 + log p).
  - Tight parity threshold tau=0.01; automatic η attenuation on repeated violations; rollback.

Code: quant-only adaptation loop

def run_quant_only_adaptation(model_wrap, sketchbank, quant_adapter, stream_iter, tau=0.01):
    adapters = {'quant': quant_adapter}
    violation_streak = 0
    rows = []
    for x, y, corr, sev in stream_iter:
        x = augment_brightness_contrast(x)
        x = add_shot_noise(x)
        x = add_outlier_patches(x)
        x, y = x.to(model_wrap.device), y.to(model_wrap.device)
        ylog_q = model_wrap.forward_quant(x)
        p = torch.softmax(ylog_q, dim=-1).squeeze(0)
        e = p * (1.0 + torch.log(torch.clamp(p, 1e-6, 1.0)))
        e_prime = sketchbank.project_error(e)
        feats = torch.zeros(5)  # no gate features needed if K=1
        ok, gap = parity_guard(model_wrap, x, bits_list=[8,6,4], tau=tau)
        if not ok:
            violation_streak += 1
            quant_adapter.lr *= 0.5
        else:
            violation_streak = 0
        snap = clone_adapter_state(adapters)
        sketchbank.apply_update(adapters, e_prime, feats)
        # rollback if parity worsens or if violation streak > 3
        ok2, gap2 = parity_guard(model_wrap, x, bits_list=[8,6,4], tau=tau)
        if (not ok2) or (gap2 > gap + 0.01) or (violation_streak >= 3):
            restore_adapter_state(adapters, snap)
            violation_streak = 0
        acc = (model_wrap.forward_quant(x).argmax(dim=-1) == y).item()
        rows.append({'corr': corr, 'sev': int(sev), 'acc': acc, 'gap': gap2})
    return rows


- Baselines
  - Static PTQ; BN-only gradient update (does not change quantization parameters); upper-bound QAT on a tiny buffer (1–2 epochs) for scales/clips only.

- Metrics
  - Accuracy recovery: before perturbation, after perturbation, after adaptation; FP–int8 gap trajectories; parity-trigger events; activation coverage (fraction of activations within 0.8×clip); time-to-recover (steps to 95% of pre-perturb accuracy).

- Ablations
  - S_q trained via OCTAV-lite vs naive proportional control: Δs = k_s·(target_coverage−observed), Δc = k_c·(percentile−clip).
  - r_e ∈ {8,16}, K ∈ {1,4} for quant group only; trust-region off vs on.

- Success criteria
  - Recover ≥70% of accuracy lost due to miscalibration within ≤500 steps; FP–int8 gap reduced by ≥50% vs no adaptation; parity guard averts divergence under severe shifts.


Experiment 3: Cross-modality validation on speech KWS (DS-CNN on SpeechCommands)
- Goal and hypothesis
  - Validate modality generality; show bias/norm + small spectral diagonals (r≤8) improve KWS under noise/RIR with tiny compute and no backprop.

- Dataset and model
  - SpeechCommands v2 (35 classes) via torchaudio.datasets.SPEECHCOMMANDS. Preprocess to 40-dim log-Mel features (25 ms window, 10 ms hop). Model: DS-CNN small (conv depthwise separable).
  - Domain shift: add background noise (MUSAN) and RIRs; SNR ∈ {0,5,10} dB.
  - Quantization: int8 weights, 8-bit activations; evaluate 6-bit via fake-quant.

Code: data and augmentation

class SpeechCommandsDataset(torchaudio.datasets.SPEECHCOMMANDS):
    def __init__(self, subset: str="training"):
        super().__init__('./data', download=True)
        def load_list(filename):
            with open(os.path.join(self._path, filename)) as f:
                return [os.path.join(self._path, line.strip()) for line in f]
        if subset == 'validation':
            self._walker = load_list("validation_list.txt")
        elif subset == 'testing':
            self._walker = load_list("testing_list.txt")
        else:
            excludes = set(load_list("validation_list.txt") + load_list("testing_list.txt"))
            self._walker = [w for w in self._walker if w not in excludes]

mel = torchaudio.transforms.MelSpectrogram(sample_rate=16000, n_fft=512, hop_length=160, n_mels=40)
amp_to_db = torchaudio.transforms.AmplitudeToDB()


def wav_to_logmel(wav):
    with torch.no_grad():
        M = mel(wav)
        return amp_to_db(M).clamp(min=-80, max=0)


def add_noise_rir(wav, noise_bank, rir_bank, snr_db=5):
    # Simple additive noise and convolution with RIR
    if rir_bank is not None:
        rir = rir_bank[torch.randint(len(rir_bank), (1,)).item()].to(wav.device)
        wav = torch.nn.functional.conv1d(wav.unsqueeze(0), rir.unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(0), padding=rir.numel()-1).squeeze(0)
    if noise_bank is not None:
        noise = noise_bank[torch.randint(len(noise_bank), (1,)).item()].to(wav.device)
        # Match length
        noise = noise[:,:wav.shape[-1]]
        s_power = wav.pow(2).mean()
        n_power = noise.pow(2).mean()
        snr_lin = 10 ** (snr_db/10)
        alpha = torch.sqrt(s_power / (snr_lin * n_power + 1e-8))
        wav = torch.clamp(wav + alpha * noise, -1.0, 1.0)
    return wav


- Adapters
  - g1: Bias+BN affine in conv blocks.
  - g2: Spectral diagonal on final conv block, r=8.
  - g3: Quant adapters on first conv activation clip and final dense layer scales.
  - Loss proxy: entropy minimization; optional EMA-teacher consistency on logits.

- Offline precompute
  - Learn P on clean train set; fit S_g using augmented audio (noise/RIR) as proxy to improve robustness.

- On-device loop
